the “well [pozzo] exercise”. A creative mental exercise using the image of a stone thrown into a well to bring the motivation for the training into consciousness.
the stabilizing and intention-setting exercise: A breathing-relaxation exercise, followed by silently formulated wishes for a loved one’s happiness and freedom from suffering; it also includes reflections about one’s purpose and aspirations in life.
the exercise in embodying compassion and other feeling-states [vivere in prima persona la compassione e altre emozioni positive]. It includes a progression of exercises:


tuning into the surrounding sounds [essere partecipi dell’ambiente fisico circostanze – suoni, luci, altre sensazioni],
remembering and recreating a positive feeling-state [internalizzare],
expressing positive feeling through a body posture;
expressing negative feeling (anger) through a body posture;
a group discussion about the exercise.


the self-acceptance and self-forgiveness exercise [auto-accettazione e dall’auto-perdono]: dyadic or solitary recollection of negative self-judgement and exploration of the needs concealed in this behavior.
the self-appreciation exercise [apprezzamento nei confronti di se stessi]: dyadic exercise training in silent, appreciative looking at one another; alternated with dialogic exercise, asking the other to express positive thoughts about themselves, listening, and thanking.
the “empathic attunement” exercise [condivisione empatica]: dyadic exercise in sharing sorrow and disappointments, and receiving and offering empathic attention.
the “eyes on” exercise [esercizio dello sguardo]: dyadic exercise in progressive steps of


looking at the other person,
cognitive perspective taking, imagining how s/he feels being seen,
considering the other as fellow human being with human sufferings,
wishing him/her well.

Each step is alternated with self-reflection; the exercise concludes with a group discussion.

the “breathe in, breathe out” exercise [Pratica del Respiro: Respiriamo ciò che è doloroso e indesiderato con il sincero desiderio che noi e gli altri potessimo essere liberi dalla sofferenza. Espirando, inviamo sollievo dal dolore con l’intenzione che noi e gli altri siamo felici.]: the practice of tonglen, practiced in dyads, facing one another, eyes closed, ending with looking into each others’ eyes.



12.3.6 The Guided Meditation
The meditation training is derived from compassion practices found in the Tibetan Buddhist traditions, while however deliberately eschewing explicit Buddhist concepts and terminology. Their content corresponds to the six-step pedagogy explained above.
The guided meditations follow the Buddhist model in that they are individual contemplative exercises. The instructor is guiding the meditation by giving verbal instructions, which is a format unknown in traditional Buddhist settings.

12.3.6.1 Interim conclusion
In sum, this description of CCT reveals a robust program for cultivating compassion that leaves little room for deviation in its didactic approach. Although the instructors are encouraged to contribute their own experience and personality, the IM guarantees a high degree of uniformity in the education of secular compassion.

title: 12  Coltivazione secolarizzata della compassione
section: 12.4 Similarities and differences between Secular and Traditional Compassion Training
text:
12.4 Similarities and differences between Secular and Traditional Compassion Training

12.4.1 Similarities
Step 1. Secular compassion training begins with learning the skill of focusing and settling the mind. The term mindfulness is often used indiscriminately for various mental states such as concentrated focus, bare attention, or open monitoring. The IM emphasises the dimension of samatha [tranquillità, ovvero calma, chiarezza ed equanimità] e delle pratiche contemplative che tendono a sviluppare questo stato mentale.
The IM explains two mental faculties, namely

mindfulness, which helps keep our attention on the chosen object of our focus, and
meta-cognition, an awareness that helps monitor whether or not our attention is still present.

CCT uses breathing techniques such as counting the breathing cycles, which are common methods for settling [rilassare] the mind.
Right from the first step of the CCT program, the trainees are asked tp continue their practice training in their day-to-day life. This is a direct reflection of the traditional Buddhist twofold aspects of

formal practice sessions,
post-sitting practices.

The latter refers to the idea of applying whatever one has learned in meditation in everyday situations. In support of this instruction, the IM even cites one of Atīśa’s mind training verses verbatim:

Relate whatever you encounter to your meditation practice right now.

The CCT then turns to its central theme of intention setting, which is a fundamental element of every traditional Buddhist practice. A common formalized way for Buddhists to recall and establish their intentions are the recitations of taking refuge and generating bodhicitta. More importantly is to mentally cultivate the related states of directing their minds towards awakening.
Another preparatory step in CCT is sharpening the awareness for suffering. The Buddhist parallel for this can be found in the contemplation known as the “four mind-changers”, which comprises contemplations on impermanence, death, the shortcoming of samsaric existence, the infallibility of karma. The purpose of all these contemplations is to create a solid motivation for spiritual practice.
Step 2 The training then focuses on loving-kindness and compassion for a loved one. Many parallels to the previously presented Lojong and Lamrim instructions are obvious. Both approaches also include the generation of associated positive emotions, such as gratitude, joy, and rejoicing in the virtue of oneself and others. However, not all Buddhist scholars favor affection and attachment-based methods.
Step 3 The CCT then teaches “loving-kindness and compassion for oneself.” Although it is possible to find associated ideas in Buddhist thought, there exists no self-standing practice of self-compassion in Buddhist traditions.
Step 4 prepares a rationale for global compassion. The challenge of developing this rationale is the fact that the traditional Buddhist ontological explanation that all beings have been mothers throughout countless lifetimes cannot be adopted by the secular CCT for its incompatibility with secularism. Instead, the IM develops the two concepts of

common humanity [Comune Umanità],
interdependence,

echoing the two pillars of the Dalai Lama’s secular ethics.

The idea of a shared humanity and of all humans equally striving for happiness draws on Śāntideva’s rationale regarding the equality of self and others.
The principle of interdependence reflects an aspect of the theory of dependent origination [coproduzione condizionata, originazione interdipendente] of Buddhist thinking. Secular version:


My life and the lives of the countless beings on this planet are intertwined in a network of relationships on which I completely depend for my survival and well-being.

In this step, the link to the Buddhist background is most clearly expressed by the emphasis on the inclusion of all beings, even one’s enemies, in universal compassion.
Step 5 CCT presents the practice of “active compassion,” which refers to “giving and receiving,” a clear adaptation of tonglen.
In sum, it is clear that, besides the explicit adoption of a formal Buddhist contemplative practice, CCT implicitely contains and adapts a large number of pedagogical elements derived from various Buddhist sources.


12.4.2 Differences

12.4.2.1 Differences in Scope
The most fundamental difference between traditional and secular approaches to compassion training is their respective final goal and scope.

Buddhist compassion cultivation is a component of a larger soteriological perspective of awakening. Karuṇā is practiced as part of bodhicitta with the goal of transcending saṃsāra and becoming the perfect embodiment of karuṇā and prajñā in the state of a buddha. Bodhisattvas practice mahākaruṇā instead of ordinary compassion. They aspire to a state in which mahākaruṇā is characterized by radical impartiality, equanimity, and the ontology of emptiness and non-duality.
Secular training eschews [evita] all aspects of spiritual liberation. The scope of CCT is much more modest. Five target-areas of transformation can be identified in CCT.

First, it aims at establishing a heightened awareness of one’s state of mind and trains the capacity of staying present with it.
Second, it seeks to transform the outlook onto the world from a selfish to a compassionate perspective.
Third, it establishes altruistic intentions, which will affect one’s motivation. Through deliberate training of altruistic concepts and intentions, one is able to transform one’s motivation.
The fourth is to broaden our range of empathy, which should extend beyond a small circle of loved ones, beyond one’s limited ingroup.
And fifth, the transformed attitude should manifest in behaviour and habit. If the training does not result in an increase of compassionate behavior, its purpose is wasted.


Generally speaking, the goal of CCT training is for trainees to recognize their innate compassionate nature and reconnect with this natural potential. Thus, CCT’s scope is less ambitious than its Buddhist antecedent, as it remains within the realm of personal development. Participants seek to improve their personal and professional relationships and to generally become happier people.
In contrast, Buddhist literature on the topic does not envision the cultivation of compassion for its own sake, or for the sake of personal health or well-being, but for the sake of attaining the lofty goal of awakening.
In sum, the most fundamental difference between the two approaches lies in their respective compassion cultures: Buddhist approaches include transcendent, soteriological, universal and trans-personal dimensions in its view, whereas secular approaches are immanent, relational, personal, and thus more modest in its ambition.






Il concetto di pratītyasamutpāda, nel canone buddhista, spiega il prodursi dei fenomeni legati all’esistenza secondo una logica di causa ed effetto. Dal Nidānasūtra leggiamo

Il Buddha disse: “Che cos’è che si chiama senso primo della Coproduzione condizionata? Perché esiste quello, esiste questo … Condizionate dall’ignoranza compaiono i coefficienti karmici; condizionata dai coefficienti compare la coscienza; condizionati dalla coscienza compaiono nome e forma; condizionati da nome e forma compaiono i sei sensi; condizionati dai sei sensi compare il contatto; condizionata dal contatto compare la sensazione; condizionata dalla sensazione compare la”brama”; condizionata dalla brama compare l’attaccamento; condizionata dall’attaccamento compare l’esistenza; condizionata dall’esistenza compare la nascita; condizionate dalla nascita compaiono vecchiaia e morte, tristezza e sofferenza. È ciò che si chiama il grande aggregato intero dei dolori. È tale ciò che si chiama il senso primo della Coproduzione condizionata.

Si noti dunque che, nei testi Buddhisti, pratītyasamutpāda ha un significato ontologico, che riguarda tutti gli aspetti dell’esistenza, non solo il piano umano/sociale, e viene concepito come un’alternativa al nostro modo di pensare “dualistico”, ovvero basato sulla formazione di categorie, cioé, astrazioni che accomunano e suddividono i fenomeni. Di tutto questo non c’è nulla nel IM.





12.4.2.2 Differences in Method
Given the different scopes of compassion cultivation, it is natural that their methods also differ.
Previously, we have discussed the constructive, deconstructive and cognitive-analytic approaches in traditional Buddhist compassion cultivation. CCT works mostly with the first and third of these: the constructive in the form of intention-setting and training altruistic attitudes; the cognitive-analytic in the form of developing self-awareness, a transformed world view, and greater knowledge of compassion science.
While Buddhist approaches also contain these elements, they place a comparatively greater emphasis than CCT on the cognitive-analytic method of investigating the suffering in all its various forms and manifestations. They also favor deconstructive methods that aim at leveling out attachments and aversions to others.
Buddhist texts vary in their degree of deconstruction, the Bodhicaryāvatāra advocating a radical breakdown of the barriers of dualistic perception, whereas Lojong and Lamrim propose moderate forms of changing one’s appreciation of self and others. These methods, however, all lead to an equanimous attitude towards all beings, which implies rejecting ingroup-outgroup thinking and giving up preferential attitutes even toward loved ones. Such radical impartiality is not a goal of secular CT.
More specifically, we can distinguish between three paires of opposites: dialogic versus introspective, emotional versus dispassionate [spassionato], and self-compassion as primary focus versus self-compassion as side-product.

12.4.2.2.1 Dialogic versus Introspective
The first contrast pertains to the level of relational dynamics in compassion cultivation.
An important experiential part of the CCT group session are the eight exercises contributed by psychotherapists to the program. Most of these exercises are dyadic and include verbal and non-verbal communication.

Whereas CCT’s dyadic exercises involve a high level of relational involvement in the form of dialogue and non-verbal communication with a physically present partner,
the contemplative exercises of equalizing and exchange or tonglen internalize the relation to others and demand introspection and even isolation.

One can say that traditional Buddhist compassion cultivation relies more heavily on methods that demand the capacity to evoke the object of compassion in one’s imagination rather than interacting with other trainees. This requires that the meditators are able to sustain introspection, control their thoughts and imagine the objects of compassion and their various forms of suffering. The relational dynamic becomes important only outside the formal sessions, when meditators have to transfer the transformed attitude to everyday life situations.


12.4.2.2.2 Emotional versus dispassionate
The empathic involvement with the suffering of others is a method for connecting with one’s inborn compassionate nature and for stimulating further cultivation of compassion. However, the method of arousing empathy and positive affects as a didactic method stands in constrast to a dispassionate understanding of conventional reality, including the dissatisfactory nature of saṃsāra, impermance, no-self and emptiness, as described in the three ālambanas of compassion or the Bodhicaryāvatāra’s deconstructive didactics.
While Buddhist approaches describe a progression that leads increasingly to a dispassionate approach, CCT makes heavily use of the power of emotions to touch participants and move them towards a transformation of mind.
Therefore, CCT’s methods are not analytical in the sense of traditional Buddhist analysis of the nature of reality, impermanence, suffering, or interdependence. Rather, CCT relies strongly on generating feelings of warmth and tenderness, evoked by recollecting specific situations with a loved one.
While many Lojong and Lamrim instructions begin with the same approach, the Buddhist instructions aim at establishing a state that transcends emotionality and is a general outlook on life based on an understanding of reality (prajñā), rather than a response to a specific sight of suffering. In the final stage, Mahākaruṇā is radically impartial, and therefore dispassionate in the sense of being equanimous and universal.


12.4.2.2.3 Self-compassion: primary focus versus as side-product
Buddhist traditions do not offer explicit practices for self-compassion. However, compassion for the self is constantly included in wishes for happiness and ultimate awakening.
In the Tibetan tradition, several prayers begin with the formula (“I and all beings”), (“I and all beings who have been my mothers”), which clearly expresses the inclusion of one’s own benefit in all spiritual practice.
The idea of a person loving and caring for themselves is taken for granted in Buddhist literature: “in order to have compassion for others it is necessary to have it for oneself”. However, in all Buddhist practices, self-compassion is cultivated simultaneously with compassion for others. Śāntideva’s Bodhicaryāvatāra even suggests that focusing on self-compassion alone is a cause of suffering:

All the misery the world contains
Has come through wanting happiness for oneself.

Therefore, it would be farfetched to claim that Buddhist literature contains instructions on cultivating self-compassion. This is however what Kristin Neff (2003) asserts when she introduces the psychological category of self-compassion as an “important Buddhist concept”.
Neff has popularized self-compassion as a distinct construct of Western psychology; she designed it as a new field of scientific research with its own self-compassion scale and developed a related training program called “Mindful Self-Compassion” (MSC).
According to her explanations, self-compassion consists of three principal facets, namely kindness, connectedness, and mindfulness, and is therefore distinct from selfishness, self-indulgence or narcissism.
While the therapy Neff developed might yield promising results, from a Buddhist studies perspective, it is inaccurate to claim that there might be a Buddhist practice with the explicit focus of training self-compassion.
On the other hand, CCT conceives self-compassion as a preparatory step to developing compassion for others, but without treating it as a separate, individual practice.
Despite the lack of Buddhist scriptural sources, the idea that there might be a Buddhist practice of self-compassion is gaining momentum.

Buddhist magazine Lion’s Roar, supports this idea;
psychologists re-interprets the Tibetan Lojong slogans, claiming that one begins with the practice of self-compassion;
there are books that present self-compassion as a Buddhist idea.

title: 12  Coltivazione secolarizzata della compassione
text:
Conclusions
Despite the many similarities between the comparison of compassion practice in the two contexts, secular and traditional Lojong and Lamrim, it is also evident that there are major difference between the two, expecially in the conceptual frameworks of their respective cultures which determines the aim of the training. While Buddhist compassion cultivation constitutes an essential part of the bodhisattva path to buddhahood, secular compassion is being trained with much more modest goals.
While bodhisattvas develop Mahākaruṇā, universal compassion, aiming at complete impartiality, secular training aims at simply connecting and reviving the natural propensity of humans for compassion, loving-kindness, and prosocial attitudes.
In sum, the previous considerations reveal that there are important differences between Buddhism as a soteriological practice, and secular CCT.
This raises the question of what it means to secularize Buddhist meditation practices. Does secularization mean eliminating Buddhist terminology, amending concepts that imply transcendence, but otherwise implying a Buddhist worldview? Or is it required, for a secular perspective, to change the Buddhist worldview? And, more importantly, does the secular approach achieve its goal?

title: 13  Il significato della secolarizzazione
text:
Nei precedenti capitoli abbiamo confrontato la tradizionale buddista e le pratiche secolarizzate di coltivazione della compassione. Abbiamo analizzato i fondamenti filosofici e psicologici sottostanti ai due approcci. Questo confronto ha rivelato una convergenza nell’idea di un potenziale naturale e innato negli esseri umani, ma anche una divergenza nella concettualizzazione delle forme avanzate di compassione. Mentre i pensatori buddisti hanno sviluppato schemi elaborati per distinguere i livelli ordinari e avanzati di compassione, il contesto secolarizzato si limita a quelli che, nella tradizione buddista, sono i livelli iniziali della compassione.
Abbiamo poi esaminato i due approcci pedagogici incorporati nelle due culture della compassione.

La cultura della compassione buddista Mahāyāna è definita dalla cosmologia buddista, una filosofia morale basata sul karma, e dalla didattica del percorso di perfezionamento spirituale del bodhisattva.
La cultura della compassione secolare è fondata su una visione scientifica dei fenomeni, su una ricerca progettata per lo sviluppo della salute individuale e, in una certa misura, su un senso etico secolare rivolto alla promozione della pace.

Il confronto delle due pedagogie della coltivazione della compassione si è concentrato sulla tecnica contemplativa del tonglen. Dalle prime scritture Mahāyāna fino alla letteratura commentata tibetana del XIV e XV secolo, questa tradizione rivela uno slancio creativo e innovativo che ha trasformato un’impegnativa meditazione dei bodhisattva avanzati in una contemplazione accessibile anche ai praticanti principianti. La tradizione tibetana ha trasformato la coltivazione della compassione che richiede la comprensione dei concetti di anātman e śūnyatā in una meditazione molto più accessibile che può essere praticata senza la necessità della decostruzione del pensiero dualistico. In questo senso, le trasformazioni tibetane del modo di intendere la compassione hanno aperto la strada alle pratiche secolarizzate occidentali.
Coerentemente con questa idea, abbiamo visto che la pedagogia CCT è infusa di idee buddiste in ogni fase del suo programma. Tuttavia, è anche chiaro che la formazione secolarizzata si distingue dalla didattica buddista in quanto enfatizza uno sviluppo personale, che non mette in discussione l’ambiente in cui si trova l’individuo, ed è molto meno ambiziosa nel suo obiettivo. Al contrario, la coltivazione della compassione buddista fa parte del sentiero del bodhisattva che conduce al completo risveglio, e non può essere concepita come un obiettivo autonomo che può essere raggiunto all’interno della sua vita sociale e lavorativa dell’individuo.
Ciò che resta da esplorare sono questioni fondamentali riguardanti le implicazioni del processo di secolarizzazione delle pratiche buddiste tradizionali per un pubblico nell’Europa e nel Nord America del ventunesimo secolo.

title: 13  Il significato della secolarizzazione
section: 13.2 The consequence of secularizing Buddhist compassion training
text:
13.2 The consequence of secularizing Buddhist compassion training
Discussions about the meaning of the secularization have multiplied in recent years. Charles Taylor’s A Secular Age describes three types of secularity, namely

the expulsion of religion from the sphere of public life, that is, from the economic, political, cultural, educational, professional, recreational spheres of activity;
secondly, the decline of religious belief and practice,
thirdly the understanding of the belief in God as a free personal choice, “one human possibility among others”.

Particularly the third variant illustrates the situation in which secular compassion training has arisen. In CCT, religious belief is optional; the program is designed to be compatible with all religious affiliations, as well as atheism and agnosticism.
“Secular” in the context of compassion training refers not to an exclusion or rejection of religion, but to an all-inclusive attitude towards religion. This all-inclusive stance is precisely the model of secularism that the Dalai Lama sees as the foundation of his secular ethics.
Religious belief is not excluded in CCT but relegated to the private sphere of the individual, as compassion is associated with a naturalistic worldview based on rationality and scientific empiricism rather than religious or moral obligation.
The motivation of secularizing compassion training did not emerge from a disenchantment with religion, but rather from the wish to make the training available for the largest possible audience.
The comparative analysis of CCT with Buddhist antecedents has shown that, while using secular language,

CCT does indeed express a worldview that is strongly influenced by Buddhist thought,
the IM communicates in large part Buddhist didactic methods without using Buddhist terminology (except for the term tonglen).

Secularizing Buddhist compassion cultivation in the case of CCT thus refers to the result of two processes.

The first is the removal of Buddhist terminology and Buddhist concepts so that the compassion training may not conflict to currently accepted scientific ideas, while keeping intact as much as possible the didactic methods developed in Buddhist traditions.
The second process is that of hybridizing: The training elements derived from sources other than Buddhism such as the psychotherapeutic and dyadic exercises, or the training in self-compassion, are inserted for the purpose of making the training “workable” for Western audiences.

title: 13  Il significato della secolarizzazione
section: 13.3 Evaluating the Secularization of Buddhist Contemplative Practices
text:
13.3 Evaluating the Secularization of Buddhist Contemplative Practices
Two critiques have been directed to the secularization of Buddhist contemplative practices.
First, the first critique concerns the fact that the Buddhist practices are built on different models of mental health than those pursued by Western audiences.






It is especially important to keep in mind this point when evaluating the effectiveness of the secularizated Buddhist contemplative practices.



Let us take the example of mental distress and depression. Early Buddhist sūtras depict suffering as an inevitable ingredient of being alive. They posite that

the only genuine remedy to suffering is escape from saṃsāra (the phenomenal world) altogether, and escape requires, among other things, abandoning hope that happiness in this world is possible.

This type of hopelessness, which in Buddhism represents a state of spiritual maturity, is regarded as a form of depression in present day culture of the West.
Our contemporary culture prefers to see spiritual practice as a “science of happiness.”
Buddhist thinkers such as Śāntideva acknowledge that suffering can serve as a catalyst for spiritual practice. Therefore, Buddhist contemplative practice does not aim at soothing short-term suffering when this pain serves eradicating the causes for long-term suffering.
Western models of mental health however tend to see distress and depression as undesirable mental states that must be remedied.
Secular compassion training, therefore, is vulnerable to the criticism that it runs the risk of being used to pacify short-term forms of suffering instead of being used as catalysts for a more profound transformation.
A second critique argues that secular programs are the product of a selective appropriation and commodification of Buddhist contemplative practices for the sole purpose of satisfying the widespread consumerism of Western societies: a watered-down version of mindfulness, […] a meditation for the masses, stripped of its essential ingredients, prepackaged and neatly stocked on the shelves of the commercial self-help supermarkets”. Commodifying Buddhist ideas for self-centred purposes entails tailoring them for the individualistic consumer orientation of Western capitalist societies instead of eradicating egocentrism, the basic root of suffering.

13.3.1 Response to the Critiques
To these critiques one could answer that CCT has no claim to authenticity in a Buddhist sense of transmission, and that its validity comes from the fact that “it works.”
The critique of selective appropriation with the purpose of commodification is the one that is more difficult to answer. However, we should note that the most important agent in such secularization project is the Dalai Lama himself. He has financially contributed to the Stanford program, regularly participates in conferences, and has, over the years, developed his secular ethics, with which he promotes compassion as a human, not a religious quality.
Moreover, it is also clear that the history of Buddhist thought shows the presence of very dramatic changes. Changes form Theravada Buddhism to Mahayana Buddhism are very extreme. In today’s discussion, we have seen the transformation from the advanced bodhisattva practice of equalizing and exchange (as described by Śāntideva in the late 7th to mid-8th century CE) to the way in which the practice of tonglen can be applied by novices’ in the Lojong and Lamrim contemplations.
Thus, the commodificaiton is only one aspect of this phenomenon. The other being the progressive transformation of Buddhist practice throughout the centuries and its ability to adapt to the needs of its host culture.
Finally, there is an inevitable transformation of religious concept when a religion starts a dialogue with science. In the present context, compassion becomes natural, psychological category, a universal quality of every human being, at the core of human nature, rather than an ethical imperative.

title: 14  The construct of compassion in psychology
text: Qual è lo status del costrutto di “compassione” nella ricerca psicologica contemporana?

title: 14  The construct of compassion in psychology
text:
14.1 Gilbert (2019)
Gilbert, P. (2019). Explorations into the nature and function of compassion. Current Opinion in Psychology, 28, 108–114.
The last 30 years has seen a considerable upsurge of research into prosocial psychology. This includes explorations of altruism, empathy, morality and ethics, and compassion.

title: 14  The construct of compassion in psychology
text:
14.2 Origins
Given that there are evolutionary origins to many of our dimensions for prosocial behaviour, one dominant source of the basis of compassion evolved from mammalian caregiving of offspring.
The essence of mammalian infant caring behaviour is to be attentive to the distress and needs of another (e.g. infant), take actions to relieve distress, fulfil appropriate needs to prevent future distress and suffering, support growth, and prepare offspring for the challenges of independent living.
However, caring for others is a costly behaviour, and therefore is generally constrained to the extent that it is more likely to be focused on kin and allies than non-kin and strangers.

title: 14  The construct of compassion in psychology
text:
14.3 From caring to compassion
What distinguishes compassion from basic nonhuman caring is that human compassion requires a particular set of cognitive competencies. They include a range of complex reasoning abilities that enable various forms of self-awareness, symbolic thinking, mentalising, reflection on the past, and behaviourally contingent predictions of the future.

title: 14  The construct of compassion in psychology
text:
14.4 Definitions
There are many different definitions of compassion. Some controversy exists over whether compassion is

an affective state that motivates compassionate behaviour,
or is a primary motive that gives rise to emotion.

title: 14  The construct of compassion in psychology
text:
14.5 Compassion as motivation
Buddhist approaches and motivational theorists focus less on emotion and more on compassion as rooted in a caring motivational system. A common theme is the engagement with distress and suffering and a wish to alleviate and prevent it.
A common definition of compassion is in terms of a motivation that orientates to “… a sensitivity to suffering in self and others with a commitment to try to alleviate and prevent it”.
The deliberate turning towards suffering therefore requires a degree of motivation, willingness, courage, and distress tolerance.
The second element, that is, the issue of alleviation and prevention, requires the developmnet of the skills to do so.

Human compassion therefore involves the motivation to learn how to be helpful (as for example in the caring professions). In the contemplative traditions that dedication is with one’s own mind, to understand the nature of mind and the illusions of self, in order to (compassionately) free others from the suffering caused by such illusions. This is called Bodhicitta. Hence, at the core of compassion is courage and dedication.


The motivation for compassion can, therefore, give rise to a sense of an identity, a self one tries to become and live by; a compassionate self.


The compassionate self would, therefore, be linked to mindfully living in such a way that one tries to be helpful and not harmful and thus regulate other competing motives within oneself.


To accomplish this, one may practice developing one’s compassionate mind, with meditations, thought and behavioural practices that stimulate various physiological mechanisms that facilitate compassion (such as the frontal cortex and vagus nerve).


Linked to a compassionate self-identity may be other dispositions that focus on helping.


Hence […] looked at this way, compassion is not a particular emotion nor is it the same as ‘caring motivation’ but represents a uniting of caring motivation with a range of complex, recently evolved, social intelligences, with emotional textures that are context dependent.


As a motivation, compassion has to compete with other motives, many of which are unconscious. These can be motives for competitive behaviour focusing on self-interest, and approval/prestige seeking within a group or only kin and reciprocal relationships, and tribal/group support. […] Part of the challenge of compassion therefore is to break through these evolved boundaries around caring and helping behaviour.

title: 14  The construct of compassion in psychology
text:
14.6 Fears blocks and resistances

As with any motivation, compassion, and some of its dependent competencies such as (empathy or distress tolerance), can be feared, blocked or resisted. Fears relate to the consequences of becoming more empathically compassionate such as being overwhelmed by distress or feeling personally undeserving.


Blocks can be related to internal processes such as maybe wanting to be compassionate but not knowing how, or a lack of understanding of compassion. Resistance is where individuals are not frightened nor blocked but simply don’t want to be compassionate. For example, thinking “why should we help immigrants? It’s too costly.”


All forms of compassion training will need to be aware of, and have means of addressing, fears, blocks, and resistances.

title: 14  The construct of compassion in psychology
text:
14.7 Compassion as a social mentality

Compassion can be understood as a reciprocal process where providers will be paying attention to the impact of their caring on others, and the receivers will be monitoring how helpful the providers are.


For these and other reasons compassion can be understood as an integrated flow involving compassion to others, becoming receptive to the compassion from others, and also self-compassion. There are psychological measures for each of these dimensions.


In some clinical contexts, individuals may be very blocked on being open to receiving compassion, fear closeness, feel isolated and lonely, and need to begin to experience compassion from a therapist before they can become self-compassionate.

title: 14  The construct of compassion in psychology
text:
14.8 Compassion, body and brain

[…] it is now known that the quality of care and compassion that humans receive have major psychological and physiological impacts.

Vengono forniti esempi di psicologia dell’età evolutiva. Il focus è la connessione con cambiamenti a livello neurale.

title: 14  The construct of compassion in psychology
text:
14.9 Compassionate mind training and therapy

Compassion cultivation is core to all the contemplative traditions. Some traditions focus on mindfulness training as a platform for insight meditation. This suggests that as the mind slows and settles it gains insight into the true nature of mind, the illusory nature of a separate self, enabling compassion to arise naturally.


In the Mahayana tradition, however compassion is a target for training in its own right.


Recently, Western science has seen a growth of research studying the impact of compassion training on a range of physiological, psychological and social processes.


Some trainings focus primarily on self-compassion or compassion to others or on integrated three flows of compassion (to self, from others, to others).


Loving-kindness meditation typically focuses on directing it to self and others including ‘difficult’ people.


Various forms of compassion training impact on various physiological systems, including the neuroendocrine and immune systems, specific neurophysiological pathways, and the autonomic nervous system.


Weng et al. found that two weeks of compassion training (focusing on benevolent wishes for family, friends and difficult people) resulted in increased altruistic behaviour in a fairness giving scenario, and changes in neurophysiological mediators.
Matos et al. found that practising compassionate mind skills for two weeks resulted in a range of beneficial psychological changes associated with well- being and also changes in heart rate variability.

In fact, there are now a range of compassion focused trainings, linked to different models, all of which have some evidence of effectiveness.
Different trainings, however have different physiological effects. Valk et al. compared three forms of training linked to:

attention and mindfulness;
socio-effective (including compassion training)
socio-cognitive (including metacognition, empathy and perspective training).

These trainings produce different neurophysiological changes according to the training engaged. This indicates that these different types of training are not neurophysiologically equivalent.
Many of the above trainings were relatively short.
In contrast, Lutz et al. compared physiological (EEG) differences between relatively naive meditators and long-term compassion meditators. Long-term meditators were able to induce sustained electroencephalographic high-amplitude, gamma band oscillations and phase synchrony during meditation. These processes enable the brain to work in synchrony rather than as a segregated set of processes (as occurs when under threat).
An implication of these results is that training over time changes the physiological architecture of the brain which makes new insights possible that may not be possible without that architecture.

In regard to developing compassion focused psychotherapies, particularly with the push for short-term therapies, it is the early days of research. Some of the methodologies are poor, the training and supervision of the therapists uncertain, fidelity to the models rarely clarified, and commitment to compassion practices, in both clients and therapists, patchy. Understandable enthusiasm must therefore be held in check until much more rigorous studies with improved practice fidelities are forthcoming. It is likely that in the years to come compassionate mind trainings and therapies will need to articulate how they can produce physiological change. Reliance on self-report and symptom change will not be enough.


There are a range of compassion focused therapies that are being developed for particular groups of individuals, such as those who have been traumatised, those with complex psychoses and depressions, eating and personality difficulties.


Importantly, clinical and nonclinical populations have different fears, blocks and resistances to compassion requiring quite different steps and strategies for stimulating and cultivating compassion motivation systems.


Importantly whether we see compassion as an emotion or a motivation has implications for compassion training in psychotherapy contexts. For example, a depressed mother may be very motivated to care for her children but is depressed partly because she has lost all feeling for them, feels disconnected and lacks a sense of an emotional bond. Compassion focused therapy would focus very much on her actual behaviours and motivation and not engage in trying to generate feelings of compassion in the first instance, because very clearly the depression has suppressed that. Trying to create compassion feeling in certain clients can make them more depressed when they struggle (and fail) to do it; compassion insight and compassionate behaviour are much easier starting places. Compassion focused therapies involve motivation switching; that is switching people out of a competitive, self-critical, shame-based, defensive motivational focus into a care and compassion focused one that organises attention, thinking behaviour and physiological processes quite differently.

title: 14  The construct of compassion in psychology
text:
Conclusions

Compassion evolved out of mammalian caring motivational system(s).
Compassion is contextually sensitive, where we are more likely to be compassionate to kin and friends than to enemies.
Compassion is related to morality. For example, moral thinking can focus on rights, justice and fairness whereas compassion typically focuses on the avoidance of causing harm and the moral obligations of helping those who are suffering or less well off than ourselves. These two dimensions of moral thinking can at times be in conflict.
At core of compassion there are courage and dedication. Compassion is not automatic but something that can be deliberately chosen and worked at with a deepening of understanding over time.


The challenge of compassion research is to how to stimulate this basic motivation in all domains of life, be it within ourselves, within our relationships, organisations and of course in politics.







Questo recente articolo, pubblicato su una prestigiosa rivista scientifica psicologica, può essere preso come un esempio di come la compassione viene intesa nella comunità psicologica.
Si notino alcuni punti che sono già stati presentati nella discussione precedente.

Riduzionismo: essendo questo un punto di vista ‘scientifico’, si deve assumere che l’origine del fenomeno oggetto di studio sia ‘fisica’, ovvero necessariamente riconducibile al cervello e ai processi evolutivi. Questo punto è chiaramente espresso nell’articolo.

L’alternativa sarebbe una maggiore enfasi sui processi culturali (ricordiamo il dibattito tra ‘cultura e natura’ anche all’interno della tradizione buddista, con una maggiore enfasi sul primo polo).

Ibridizzazione: L’autore riconosce che il costrutto contiene una componente culturale che, in parte, viene attribuita alla tradizione Buddhista (ma c’è anche un riferimento alla cultura delle Hawaii che non ho citato, tra le altre). Le connessioni tra l’assunto fondazionale ‘neuro-evolutivo’ e queste influenze culturali estremamente distanti le une dalle altre non viene sviluppato: non si capisce come le dimensioni cultura/natura interagiscano, né come le diverse dimensioni culturali interagiscono tra loro.
Validità: Si prende atto che in domini diversi (natura/cultura) si possono trovare riferimenti al costrutto di interesse, ma non si esplicita il meccanismo psicologico, se c’è, che produce la manifestazione di questo fenomeno psicologico, dato che di questo gli psicologi si occupano. La validità di un costrutto corrisponde alla chiara individuazione del corrispettivo empirico del costrutto – il costrutto è un modello teorico di un fenomeno empirico. L’articolo non chiarisce qual è il fenomeno psicologico/empirico a cui il costrutto di “compassione” fa riferimento. È qualcosa di già presente che non si manifesta sempre nella maniera ottimale? È qualcosa che “deve essere coltivato” (si veda la grande enfasi sull’aspetto contestuale della compassione)? Se non è chiaro di cosa stiamo parlando, non possiamo né misurare “l’efficacia degli interventi”, né progettare “interventi psicologici”, dato che non è chiaro ciò su cui si deve intervenire.
Self-compassion: si noti la grande enfasi sulla dimensione della self-compassion. Questo sembra essere il contributo maggiore della psicologia allo studio e agli interventi in questo dominio. Ricordiamo la controversia che è associata alla self-compassion:

viene presentata come un concetto di origine Buddhista, ma non lo è.
Questo però non è un problema, dato che non c’è alcuna necessità di essere “fedeli” alla tradizione Buddhista. Il punto è che questa enfasi sull’ego (e sul suo benessere) va nella direzione diametralmente opposta ai contenuti della tradizione Buddhista, per cui è inappropriato descriverlo in quei termini. Semplificando, secondo il Buddhismo, la ricerca del benessere personale è la causa prima della sofferenza. La ricerca del benessere personale non può che portare alla sofferenza. Il percorso alternativo che viene proposto è quello della riuncia all’ego (in varie misure) e all’apertura alla compassione. Indirettamente, questo produce anche il benessere personale, ma solo come by-product, non come obiettivo primario.
In conclusione, la psicologia e l’approccio secolare propongono una serie di tecniche mirate allo sviluppo del benessere personale. Tuttavia, il Buddhismo dice chiaramente che qualsiasi tentativo di migliorare il benessere personale finisce inevitabilmente per sortire il risultato opposto. Quindi in un certo senso, la tradizione Buddhista fa la predizione che il percorso “secolarizzato” non funzionerà. D’altra parte, il benessere personale è anche una caratteristica ambita dal pensiero Buddhista. Ma non come obiettivo in sé, ma solo come prova che un altro tipo di percorso di cambiamento personale “funziona”, ovvero raggiunge obiettivi desierabili. Questo tipo di “tensione” tra gli obiettivi dell’approccio secolarizzato e gli assunti della tradizione Buddhista sono un aspetto su cui non ho trovato una riflessione nella ricerca e pratica psicologica.

title: 15  A Meta-Analysis of Compassion-Based Interventions
text: Andando più nei dettagli, esaminiamo una sintesi della letteratura psicologica fornita da una meta-analisi. Diventa così possibile capire quali sono i temi che hanno avuto maggiore risonanza.

title: 15  A Meta-Analysis of Compassion-Based Interventions
text:
15.1 Kirby, Tellegen, and Steindl (2017)
Consideriamo qui la meta-analisi più citata, ovvero:

Kirby, J. N., Tellegen, C. L., & Steindl, S. R. (2017). A meta-analysis of compassion-based interventions: Current state of knowledge and future directions. Behavior Therapy, 48(6), 778-792.


15.1.1 Introduction
Over the last 20 years, research has shown a number of benefits and positive associations of compassion for our physiological health, including

influencing genetic expression in cross-sectional studies (e.g., Fredrickson et al., 2013),
intervention studies (Klimecki, Leiberg, Ricard, & Singer, 2014),
mental health and emotion regulation (e.g., MacBeth & Gumley, 2012),
interventions on interpersonal and social relationships (e.g., Yarnell & Neff, 2013).

In light of significant positive associations and benefits associated with compassion, a number of compassion-based interventions have been developed that specifically aim to cultivate compassion.


15.1.2 Definition
Definitions of compassion vary, with some defining it as an emotion, others as a multidimensional construct, and others as a motivational system.
Geshe Thupten Jinpa, who developed the Stanford Compassion Cultivation Training program, defines compassion as being a complex multidimensional construct that is comprised of four key components:

an awareness of suffering (cognitive component),
sympathetic concern related to being emotionally moved by suffering (affective component),
a wish to see the relief of that suffering (intentional component),
a readiness to help relieve that suffering (motivational component).

Paul Gilbert, who developed Compassion-Focused Therapy, defines compassion as

the sensitivity to suffering in self and others (engagement), with a commitment to try to alleviate and prevent it (action).

In a recent review, Strauss and colleagues (2016) suggested that compassion includes five elements:

recognizing suffering;
understanding the universality of suffering in human experience;
feeling empathy for the person suffering and connecting with the distress (emotional resonance);
tolerating uncomfortable feelings aroused in response to the suffering person (e.g., distress, anger, fear);
motivation to act/acting to alleviate suffering.

The notion of self-compassion has received increasing attention with the work of Kristen Neff, who defined self-compassion, based on her interpretations of Buddhist teachings, as having three components (Neff, 2003):

being mindful, rather than overidentifying with problems;
connecting with others, rather than isolating oneself;
adopting an attitude of self-kindness, rather than being judgmental.

Given the differing definitions of compassion, it is not surprising that several different interventions have been developed to help cultivate compassion for self and others.


15.1.3 Current compassion-based interventions
It is possible to identify at least six currently empirically supported interventions that focus on the cultivation of compassion:

Compassion Focused Therapy (CFT; Gilbert, 2014),
Mindful Self-Compassion (MSC; Neff & Germer, 2013),
Compassion Cultivation Training (CCT; Center for Compassion and Altruism Research and Education, 2015);
Cognitively-Based Compassion Training (CBCT; Pace et al., 2009),
Cultivating Emotional Balance (CEB; Kemeny et al., 2012), and
Loving-Kindness (LKM) and Compassion Meditations (CM; e.g., Wallmark, Safarzadeh, Daukantaite, & Maddux, 2013).

Although all these interventions are secular in their design, theoretically these interventions have been typically influenced by Tibetan Buddhist traditions and perspectives of human suffering.
CFT is notably different from the other interventions, as the theoretical underpinning also includes a combination of evolutionary psychology, attachment theory, and social mentality theory.
All six forms of interventions have been subject to the gold- standard evaluations of randomized controlled trials (RCTs).
[…] it remains unknown whether the evidence base underpinning compassion-based interventions demonstrates reductions in suffering and improvements in mental health.






Si noti che questi sono gli espliciti obiettivi della coltivazione della compassione, dal punto di vista della psicologia.





15.1.4 Aim
The objective of this meta-analysis is to synthesize for the very first time the impacts of all compassion-based interventions in order to best understand their overall effectiveness.
Studies included in this review were RCTs, involving adults wherein cultivating compassion towards self or others was a key component in the intervention.
The first aim was to evaluate the success of compassion-based interventions using meta-analytic techniques on the following seven outcome variables:

compassion,
self-compassion,
mindfulness,
depression,
anxiety,
psychological distress,

well-being.







Si noti che i primi 3 sono possibili cambiamenti “intrinseci” della coltivazione della compassione; gli ultimi 4 sono “estrinseci”. Tutte e 4 le variabili di outcome estrinseche considerano un amento di “benessere soggettivo” come lo standard di successo dell’intervento. Paradossalmente, dal punto di vista della tradizione Mahayana degli esordi, questo “successo” dovrebbe invece essere interpretato come un fallimento, ovvero come un rafforzamento dell’ego, quando il vero obiettivo è l’opposto.



The second aim was to conduct moderator analyses to examine impact of potential variables on outcomes, including gender, age, intervention length, and so on.

However, it should be noted that this aim was not able to be achieved due to a lack of available data.

The effect sizes were standardized mean differences, represented by d, and can be interpreted using Cohen’s (1992) guidelines of small (0.2), medium (0.5), and large (0.8) effects.
Effect sizes were calculated based on the pre-post change in the treatment group means minus the pre-post change in the control group means, divided by the pooled pre-intervention standard deviation.
We performed analysis of compassion-based interventions compared to wait-list control conditions (n = 17), and then with the inclusion of the active comparison conditions (n = 21).
All studies were conducted within a 12-year period (2005–2017). A total of 1,285 participants were included in the trials with sample sizes ranging from 24 to 228.


15.1.5 Results

15.1.5.1 Comparison with a waiting-list

compassion, d = 0.55, k = 4, 95% CI [0.33-0.78],
self-compassion, d = 0.70, k = 13, 95% CI [0.53-0.87],
mindfulness, d = 0.54, k = 6, 95% CI [0.38-0.71],
depression, d = 0.64, k = 9, 95% CI [0.45-0.82],
anxiety, d = 0.49, k = 9, 95% CI [0.30-0.69],
psychological distress, d = 0.47, k = 14, 95% CI [0.19-0.56].



15.1.5.2 Comparison with an active control group
Comparison with a group involving some form of intervention.

self-compassion, d = 0.60, k = 16, 95% CI [0.33-0.78],
mindfulness, d = 0.46, k = 8, 95% CI [0.28-0.65],
depression, d = 0.62, k = 10, 95% CI [0.44-0.80],
anxiety, d = 0.42, k = 10, 95% CI [0.19-0.64],
well-being, d = 0.48, k = 9, 95% CI [0.28-0.67],
psychological distress, d = 0.40, k = 16, 95% CI [0.23-0.57].




15.1.6 Conclusions

First, the current evidence base for compassion-based intervention is small.

Second, the moderate effect sizes across outcomes demonstrated the potential impact of compassion-based interventions; however, this finding is limited to largely nonclinical populations.
Third, there was great variability in the outcomes measured, with a few RCTs using compassion-based self-report questionnaires.
Fourth, there is a need for greater methodological rigor and improved reporting in this intervention field.

When considering these findings in the context of the theoretical underpinnings of compassion, it becomes clear that there is still a lack of clarity and agreed-upon processes on how to best define and measure this construct.
For example, the interventions examined had varying definitions of compassion and focused on different types of compassion (e.g., self or other), that is

with Mindful Self-Compassion adopting a self-compassion approach as defined by Neff (2003),
Compassion Cultivation Training using a multiconstruct definition of compassion defined by Jinpa (2015), focusing largely on guided meditations to cultivate compassion (Jazaieri et al., 2013),
other interventions with no formal meditation/mindfulness components that instead focused on psychoeducation, writing tasks (e.g., letters), self-reflections, and imagery exercises to cultivate compassion.

A clearer focus on the processes underpinning these interventions is necessary to understand the actual impact of the “compassion” elements within interventions.

title: 16  Measuring the effects of psychological interventions
text:
First, let us consider the issue of measurement, from a psychological standpoint. Meaurements of psychological phenomena have different dimensions:

measure development and validation:

reliability,

title: 16  Measuring the effects of psychological interventions
text:
16.2 Mindfulness and accemptance-based treatments
MABTs promote a suite of conceptually coherent strategies designed to promote skillful responses to, and adaptive behavioral choices, in the face of challenging internal experiences (thoughts, emotions, and sensations (Keng et al., 2015; Norton et al., 2015).
Mindfulness represents one core set of strategies. These strategies seek to train dimensions of mindfulness, which include living in the present moment, as well as awareness of, labeling of, nonreactivity to, and nonjudgment of internal and external stimuli.
MABTs promote a willingness to experience all aspects of one’s present experience (acceptance) in the service of meeting one’s immediate goals and ultimate personal values.
MABTs also feature metacognitive and decentering strategies that help the patient recognize, in real time, that internal experiences are simply transient neural phenomena (versus an absolute arbiter of “truth”).
MABTs include a number of specific treatments components.

acceptance and commitment therapy (ACT) is a therapeutic approach that uses acceptance and mindfulness processes as well as values-guided commitment and behavioral change processes to enhance psychological flexibility and valued living.
Mindfulness-based cognitive therapy (MBCT) teaches practices that foster acceptance and decentering from thoughts, emotions, and physical sensations. MBCT has a heavy focus on mindfulness meditation practice, during which patients are asked to turn toward and accept any discomfort that arises, but also use the cognitive strategy of decentering from these internal experiences.
Dialectical behavior therapy (DBT) encompasses the dialectic of acceptance and change. It has four skill modules, two of which are primarily focused on acceptance (mindfulness, distress tolerance) and two of which are primarily focused on change (interpersonal effectiveness, emotion regulation).

Let us consider some examples.

16.2.1 ACT for anxiety and OCD
Many different types of mindfulness and acceptance-based interventions have been tested for anxiety disorders and OCD. However, ACT and its variants appear to represent the most commonly applied mindfulness and acceptance-based psychotherapies for anxiety disorders and OCD.
ACT treatments for anxiety disorders and OCD aim to reduce experiential avoidance by cultivating the ability to acknowledge, notice, and willingly experience thoughts, feelings, urges, and sensations associated with anxiety and fear (e.g., Twohig et al., 2018) and to more flexibly and objectively related to anxiety-related cognitions.
ACT treatments for anxiety disorders “also draw on personal values to help clients deliberately engage with feared and avoided, but valued, contexts and stimuli.”
Scholars concluded that ACT was similarly effective as cognitive behavioral treatments, g = .00 (k = 5, 95% CI [–0.24, 0.25]) for OCD. Similar conclusions were drawn in a meta-analysis of diverse ACT trials that showed no differences between ACT and classic CBT for anxiety outcomes, g = .14 (k = 9, 95% CI [–0.18, 0.45]).
Mindfulness and acceptance-based interventions for anxiety disorders (and related symptoms), and particularly ACT and its variants, represent viable treatment approaches that shows similar efficacy to classic CBT and have been successfully delivered in multiple formats (individual, group, therapist-guided online, bibliotherapy). The number and size of trials of mindfulness and acceptance-based interventions for anxiety disorders does not yet match that of classic CBT, which has been evaluated in large trials for anxiety disorders over a significantly longer period of time. Nonetheless, we conclude that ACT provides an important treatment approach for anxiety disorders for at least three reasons.

First, ACT provides an important behavioral option for treating anxiety disorders. Thus, ACT provides patients and therapists with choice regarding behavioral approaches to treatment anxiety disorders, as some clients and therapists may resonate more with one behavioral approach than another. Just as psychiatric medications offer multiple medications within the same medication class, which offers patients and providers a sense of choice to select one according to its particular characteristics, and options if one fails, so too do anxiety disorder and OCD patients and providers benefit from choice regarding behavioral approaches to treatment.
Second, many people with anxiety disorders and OCD do not respond to CBT (Loerinc et al., 2015). ACT provides one important alternative. Nascent evidence in panic disorder shows that adults who failed to improve after a course of CBT show significant improvement after ACT. Relatedly, nascent evidence suggests that baseline client characteristics predict different outcomes in ACT and CBT, and that treatment matching may be possible. Thus, in addition to providing choice, some clients respond better to ACT than to classic CBT.
Third, the ACT theoretical model has expanded the field’s understanding of the core processes involved in the development and maintenance of anxiety disorders. Specifically, theory-derived ACT processes (cognitive defusion, acceptance, experiential avoidance) have been shown to mediate outcomes from both ACT and other evidence-based behavioral therapies for anxiety disorders, including classic CBT and cued relaxation. ACT processes also predict clinically relevant responses to anxiety in laboratory studies (e.g., Campbell-Sills et al., 2005; Eifert & Heffner, 2003), suggesting that they are likely involved in the development of anxiety disorders as well as their maintenance (Forsyth et al., 2006). In summary, mindfulness- and acceptance-based psychotherapy approaches, most prominently ACT, have made important theoretical and applied contributions to the treatment of “made important theoretical and applied contributions to the treatment of anxiety disorders and OCD.

text:
16.3 Eichholz, Schwartz, Meule, Heese, Neumüller, and Voderholzer (2020)
Consideriamo un esempio di una recente ricerca nella quale una componente della compassione, ovvero la compassione verso di sè, viene messa in relazione con l’OCD. Si noti l’enfasi sulla self-compassion.

Eichholz, A., Schwartz, C., Meule, A., Heese, J., Neumüller, J., & Voderholzer, U. (2020). Self‐compassion and emotion regulation difficulties in obsessive–compulsive disorder. Clinical psychology & psychotherapy, 27(5), 630-639.

title: 16  Measuring the effects of psychological interventions
text:
16.4 The Self-compassion Scale
L’applicazione più popolare del concetto di compassione nella letteratura psicologica corrisponde al concetto di self-compassion proposto da Neff. La quale propone anche un intervento basato su questo concetto.
